summary: Page objects are a design pattern that wraps HTML pages or UI fragments with an application-specific API, shielding tests from brittle dependencies on UI implementation details. The pattern allows test code to interact with pages at a meaningful abstraction level—using strings for text fields, booleans for checkboxes, and action-oriented methods for buttons—rather than manipulating HTML directly. Fowler advocates for assertion-free page objects that provide access to page state while leaving assertion logic to test scripts. The pattern applies beyond web testing to any UI technology and represents a classic application of encapsulation that improves both maintainability and test readability.
key_points: Page objects should model what makes sense to users, not mirror the technical structure of pages—often meaning multiple page objects per page for significant UI elements
A good rule of thumb: imagine changing the concrete control; if your page object interface would need to change, the abstraction is leaky
Page objects should return fundamental types or other page objects, never exposing raw WebDriver or HTML manipulation to tests
Assertions belong in test scripts, not page objects—duplication concerns can be addressed through assertion libraries instead
Patterns that move logic out of UI (Presentation Model, Passive View) reduce the need for page objects by making UI testing less necessary
money_quotes: The basic rule of thumb for a page object is that it should allow a software client to do anything and see anything that a human can.
If you have WebDriver APIs in your test methods, You're Doing It Wrong.
A good rule of thumb is to imagine changing the concrete control - in which case the page object interface shouldn't change.
Page objects are a classic example of encapsulation - they hide the details of the UI structure and widgetry from other components.

The name 'page object' is misleading because it makes you think you should have just one page object per page. Something like 'panel object' would be better - but the term 'page object' is what's become accepted.
Ask yourself 'how can I hide some details from the rest of the software?'
It makes the client (test) code easier to understand because the logic there is about the intention of the test and not cluttered by UI details.
key_insight: Page objects apply encapsulation to UI testing by hiding structural details behind an application-meaningful API, making tests both more maintainable and more expressive of their actual intent.
